parents.father_research_notes: Per Lockwood genealogy + EH. Wikipedia says mother was 'Mary Hawley' — discrepancy with Lockwood genealogy 'Abigail (Hawley) Lockwood.' Using genealogy/EH as higher-priority source.

notable.notable_blurb: Nine-term Connecticut legislator; Revolutionary War captain

military_service.unit: First Company, Colonel Charles Webb's Seventh Connecticut Regiment
military_service.notes: Enlisted Jul 12, 1775; discharged Dec 24, 1775. Assigned to Gen. Sullivan's Brigade on Winter Hill. Assistant Commissary of Issues 1778; captain of coast guards 1780.

career.notes: Posted $5,000 bond to Henry Laurens, President of the Continental Congress, Jul 21, 1778.
Represented Norwalk nine sessions: May/Oct 1790, May/Oct 1791, Oct 1794, May/Oct 1795, May/Oct 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost home when British torched Norwalk, 1779
narrative_blocks.body: British forces burned Norwalk in July 1779, destroying Lockwood's home among hundreds of others along the shore. He had already served in the 7th Connecticut Regiment and organized a petition to the General Assembly for an armed sloop after British ships raided the harbor at night.

narrative_blocks.header: Organized Norwalk's first volunteer fire department
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Lockwood organized the first voluntary fire department in Norwalk, building civic infrastructure in the town the British had burned. He went on to represent Norwalk nine terms in the Connecticut General Assembly between 1790 and 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned Assembly for armed sloop; British still came
narrative_blocks.body: In October 1777, Lockwood joined neighbors in petitioning the Connecticut General Assembly for an armed sloop to patrol Long Island Sound after British vessels repeatedly raided Norwalk's harbor at night, firing on houses and stealing livestock. Two years later, the raids he had warned against culminated in the burning of the town that destroyed his home.

parents.father_name: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk CT; d. 1756. His estate included enslaved people.

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at six; married the town's future captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Susannah Selleck died in December 1749 when Susannah was barely six, and her father Capt. Joseph St. John died in 1756 when she was thirteen, leaving her to grow up in Norwalk without either parent. She married Captain Eliphalet Lockwood at thirty-three — he would go on to lose his home to British torches and organize the city's first volunteer fire department.
Susannah died young; the year was not recorded. Her parents gave the name to a sister born four years later.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Hooker to honor the family's descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Rev. Hooker; lived three months
narrative_blocks.body: Born in April 1801, this infant was named Hooker to declare the family's descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker — one of the few given names that announced the Hooker lineage without carrying the surname. He died in July 1801, three months old.

cross_connections.display_label: Married her second cousin — both descend from H00253 Susannah Selleck

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-nine, three children under six
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Lockwood St. John died in September 1832 at twenty-nine, leaving three children — Susanna, Charlotte, and George Buckingham — all under six years old. Her husband George St. John would remarry within two years, and her son George Buckingham would grow up to marry his own first cousin, Hannah Selleck Lockwood.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave — Town House Hill Cemetery, Norwalk CT

cross_connections.display_label: Married second cousin Polly Esther — both descend from H00253

bio.notes: Middle name honors maternal grandfather's family
bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Col. Buckingham St. John Lockwood

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave — East Norwalk Historical Cemetery. Died Feb 10, 1850 aged 75.

narrative_blocks.header: St. John and Lockwood families intermarried each generation
narrative_blocks.body: The Lockwood family historian documented that St. Johns and Lockwoods had intermarried in nearly every generation since the founding of Norwalk. Buckingham's own mother was Susannah St. John, making his 1808 marriage to Polly Esther St. John the continuation of a dynastic alliance running across at least four consecutive generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Last witness to where Norwalk's first church stood
narrative_blocks.body: By 1847, at seventy-three, Buckingham was quoted by Norwalk's city historian on the exact location of the town's original meeting-house — a site he could still picture as open common ground. He was among the last living residents with a direct memory of colonial Norwalk's original layout.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mary Esther (St. John) Lockwood

cross_connections.display_label: Born St. John; wed Lockwood second cousin in 1808

narrative_blocks.header: Born St. John; wed a Lockwood second cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Polly Esther St. John married Buckingham St. John Lockwood in 1808 — her second cousin through their common great-grandmother Susannah Selleck St. John of Norwalk, continuing the St. John-Lockwood intermarriage pattern across yet another generation. Her father William Saint John and his grandmother Susannah Saint John Lockwood were siblings.
William died young; the year was not recorded. His parents gave the name to a brother born two years later.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Frederick St. John Lockwood (Yale 1849)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1849; Norwalk banker, railroad president, legislator

education.notes: A.B. 1849. Classmate of Timothy Dwight (future 8th Yale president). Captain of the Augusta boat race crew — credited as founding vessel of the Yale Navy.

career.organization: Union Army — staffs of Maj.-Gen. King and Maj.-Gen. Russell
career.notes: Civil War service details not fully documented.

military_service.unit: Staffs of Major-Generals King and Russell
military_service.notes: Specific unit and rank not documented in Lockwood genealogy or Patch.com biography.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale A.B. 1849; classmate of Timothy Dwight, future 8th Yale president.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale Class of 1849; Dwight became the eighth university president

narrative_blocks.body: In August 1845, Lockwood captained the Augusta, an eight-oared cedar shell his classmates bought for $170, later identified as the vessel that launched what became the Yale Navy. He graduated with the class of 1849 alongside Timothy Dwight, who would become the university's eighth president.

narrative_blocks.header: Staffed two Union generals; built Norwalk's banking empire
narrative_blocks.body: After serving on the staffs of Major-Generals King and Russell in the Civil War, Lockwood became president of Fairfield County National Bank and the Danbury and Norwalk Railroad, and served three terms as a Republican representative in the Connecticut General Assembly.

narrative_blocks.header: SAR member; great-grandfather burned out by British
narrative_blocks.body: A century after British forces torched Norwalk and destroyed his great-grandfather's home, Lockwood joined the Connecticut Society of the Sons of the American Revolutionary War, a membership whose meaning ran close to home. He was also made a Knight Templar and remained active in the Norwalk Club until his final years.

bio.notes: First name "Hooker" from maternal uncle Hooker St. John (HD0367); middle name "St. John" from mother's birth family

naming_inspiration.description: First name honors his grandfather Captain Eliphalet Lockwood of Norwalk.
First name honors descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford, through his mother Susannah's Hooker line.
Middle name honors the maternal Saint John surname — his mother Susannah's birth family of Norwalk.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave — Town House Hill Cemetery, Norwalk CT

narrative_blocks.header: Three family lines compressed into one name
narrative_blocks.body: Named Eliphalet for his grandfather Captain Eliphalet Lockwood, Hooker to mark descent from Hartford's founder Rev. Thomas Hooker, and Saint John for his mother's birth family, this boy carried three generations of family identity in a single name. He died in Norwalk at sixteen, unmarried.

burialCemetery.name: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery
burialCemetery.access_notes: Sometimes locked; managed by local taxing district
features: First Settlers of Norwalk Memorial (lists 31 individuals from 1649)
Grave of Thomas 'Yankee Doodle' Fitch IV (1724-1795)
burialCemetery.notes: Oldest cemetery in Norwalk; triangle-shaped; near East Norwalk railway station. Mary Willet Hooker Buckingham buried here, near center of burying ground near East Norwalk railway station.
